Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty unconventional. I'd spend long hours, sometimes 34 hours a week, but not always in front of the LMS. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd record lectures or find podcasts and listen to them while commuting or doing chores. I'd block out big chunks of time on weekends to really dive deep into the material, often with headphones on, pacing around my apartment while listening to explanations.
Q2: My pattern varied quite a bit depending on what was going on. I'd have weeks where I was incredibly disciplined, studying at the same times every day, and then other weeks where anxiety would hit and I'd have to work around that. Deadlines definitely pushed me to cluster my efforts, but I tried to maintain some consistency because unpredictability made my anxiety worse. The structure helped me feel more in control even when I was scared about my performance.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check deadlines or download materials I could use offline. Sometimes I'd log in just to make sure I hadn't missed an announcement because the fear of missing something important was always there. Honestly, the LMS wasn't where I did most of my actual learning—it was more like a checkpoint to grab what I needed and then study elsewhere.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way my audio recordings or my notes did. I'd go through phases where I'd try to engage more with discussion posts, but it always felt a bit forced. My routine was built around listening and processing information auditorily, and the LMS just didn't support that learning style very well for me.
Q5: The lecture recordings and any audio materials were absolutely essential for me. I'd download them and listen multiple times until concepts clicked. Discussion posts were occasionally useful when someone explained something in a way that made sense to me—hearing different perspectives helped. The video content was good too because I could focus on the audio portion while taking notes.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials in their written form and would instead find audio versions or read them aloud to myself. Some of the interactive quizzes felt tedious because they didn't match how I process information. I'd delay discussion posts because the anxiety of putting my thoughts out there publicly was overwhelming, even though I knew participating would help my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could tell it would directly help me understand the material better, especially if I could engage with it auditorily. If I could listen to it, discuss it verbally with someone, or record myself explaining it, it felt valuable. Activities that were purely visual or required silent reading felt like they took more energy for less payoff for someone like me.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a source of anxiety for me, honestly. I'd worry that I was missing something important by not doing them, but then I'd also feel overwhelmed trying to fit them in. Required activities had clear expectations, which actually helped reduce my anxiety because I knew exactly what I needed to do. The ambiguity of optional work was harder to manage mentally.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on repeat during my commute or while exercising. I'd also record myself summarizing concepts and play those back. I used external podcasts related to course topics and would discuss material with study partners over coffee—none of which showed up in the LMS at all.
Q10: There were countless times when I was deeply engaged with the material but my LMS activity was minimal. I'd spend hours listening to downloaded lectures while walking, or I'd be in a study group verbally working through problems together. I also did a lot of learning by explaining concepts out loud to myself, which is how I process best. The LMS would just show I logged in once to download something.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio, and then listen to it maybe ten or fifteen times over the following weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but that one download represented hours and hours of actual engagement. Same with PDFs—I'd download them once and then use text-to-speech to listen to them repeatedly.
Q12: There were definitely moments when my anxiety spiked and engaging felt almost impossible. When I was scared about upcoming exams or worried I wasn't understanding something, I'd sometimes freeze up and avoid the LMS entirely because logging in reminded me of all the things I was anxious about. The CBT sessions I had really helped me work through those moments and get back on track.
Q13: Fatigue and anxiety definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was exhausted, the last thing I wanted was to stare at a screen and navigate through modules. My anxiety sometimes made me avoid logging in because I was scared of seeing bad grades or realizing I'd missed something. I'd compensate by doing more offline work where I felt safer and more in control of my environment.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through the LMS feeling completely disconnected, just going through the motions. I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because my mind was racing with anxious thoughts. In those moments, I'd have to step away and come back to the material in a different format—usually audio—when I could actually focus.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because putting my thoughts out publicly triggered a lot of anxiety for me. I knew they were important for my grade, but the fear of being judged or saying something wrong was paralyzing. I'd also avoid certain assignments when I felt scared about not understanding the material well enough, even though avoiding them only made the anxiety worse.
Q16: Deadlines created this intense pressure that both motivated me and terrified me. I'd often do a burst of LMS activity right before something was due, downloading everything I needed and then working intensely offline. The anxiety around deadlines was overwhelming sometimes, but my resilience kicked in and I'd push through. I learned to use that pressure productively, even when I was scared.
Q17: Feedback had a huge impact on me, probably more than it should have. Positive feedback would boost my confidence and make me want to engage more, while critical feedback would send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd avoid the LMS for a few days. I had to work through those reactions in my CBT sessions and learn to see feedback as helpful rather than threatening.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat rigid, which actually helped my anxiety because I knew what to expect. However, it wasn't designed for auditory learners, so I had to create my own parallel structure around listening and verbal processing. The clear deadlines and organized modules gave me a framework, but I had to adapt everything to fit how I actually learn best.
Q19: The heavy reliance on written materials and visual content made engagement harder for me as an auditory learner. Having to read long texts on screen was exhausting and not how I retain information. What made it easier was when there were recorded lectures or when instructors provided audio explanations—those were gold for me. More audio options would have made a huge difference.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the LMS without actually being able to focus and complete work. They might be logging in repeatedly out of fear of missing something, downloading materials over and over, or just scrolling without the mental capacity to engage productively. Anxiety can create a lot of activity that doesn't translate to output.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I earned a distinction because my real learning happened offline. I was listening to lectures on repeat, discussing concepts verbally, recording myself explaining things—all invisible to the system. Low LMS activity doesn't mean low engagement; it might just mean someone's learning style doesn't match the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show login patterns and what resources were accessed, which gives some indication of engagement timing. But they completely miss the depth and quality of learning that happens offline. They can't capture how many times I listened to a downloaded lecture, or the hours I spent in study groups, or the mental processing I did while walking. They show the surface, not the substance.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense to me, not just showing up or clicking through modules. It's about that moment when concepts click and I can explain them out loud without looking at notes. For me, engagement is deeply personal and often invisible—it happens in my headphones, in conversations, in the moments when I'm pacing and thinking through problems.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how hard I fought through anxiety to succeed in this course, or how the CBT sessions helped me develop resilience. It wouldn't capture the hours of audio learning, the verbal processing with study partners, or the emotional journey of being scared but pushing through anyway. My distinction grade came from work that was mostly invisible to any tracking system.
